core_intuitions: The KV cache, not the weights, is the dynamic bottleneck; pre-paging engines waste 60-80% of KV memory to internal + external fragmentation, and paging via a per-sequence block table cuts waste to at most one partial block per sequence while allowing non-contiguous physical placement.

vLLM's scheduler runs continuous batching under a FIXED KV-block budget: it admits/retires requests at token granularity, grows each running sequence by at most one block per decode step, and when the block pool is exhausted it must evict a WHOLE sequence (recompute or swap) - and oversubscription turns this into preemption thrashing that wrecks tail latency.

Chunked prefill / the V1 unified token budget: a single forward pass packs a slice of one long prefill together with many 1-token decodes so a long prompt no longer stalls everyone's inter-token latency (head-of-line blocking) and arithmetic intensity rises.

Automatic prefix caching falls out of the paged design: a rolling per-block hash (this block's tokens + hash of all preceding blocks) makes identical prefixes map to identical physical blocks, so a shared prefix is reused by pointer + refcount bump up to the first divergent block, then only the remaining tokens are prefilled.

The engine/executor/worker stack separates orchestration (scheduler + block manager + request lifecycle) from execution (per-GPU model shard + attention backend), and V1 isolates the engine loop in its own process to overlap CPU work with GPU execution.

Block-manager mechanics - a refcounted free list, copy-on-write on shared blocks, and lazy incremental growth (pop a new block only at a block boundary) - are what make sharing and near-zero internal fragmentation possible.

existing.name: vllm-paged-block-table-mapping
existing.issue: The core mapping (logical KV -> block_table -> non-contiguous physical blocks, two sequences interleaved) reads clearly and the metaphor is strong. But in the DARK theme the two bottom caption lines ('Uniform block size...', 'Last block partially filled...') are near-black-on-black and barely legible, and the trailing '...' on the right sits at the viewBox edge and clips.
existing.improvement: Recolor the two caption lines to a theme-aware muted-foreground token (not a hardcoded near-black) so they read in dark mode, and add right-side margin / shorten so no text touches or crosses the viewBox edge. Optionally add one tiny visual cue that a physical block can be pointed to by BOTH block tables (the copy-on-write / shared-prefix case) - e.g. a faint dashed arrow from both block_table[A] and block_table[B] converging on a single shared block with a small 'refcount 2' tag - but only if it can be added without crowding; keep the current clean layout as the priority.

existing.name: vllm-engine-executor-stack
existing.issue: The layered structure (API -> LLMEngine/EngineCore -> Executor -> Workers/ModelRunner) is the right decomposition, but there are genuine overlap/clipping defects: the multi-line inter-layer arrow labels ('ExecuteModelRequest (token ids, block tables, sampling params)') overlap the border of the box directly below them in BOTH themes, and the 'V1: dedicated process' pill clips the right viewBox edge in the dark render.
existing.improvement: Increase the vertical gap between the LLMEngine box and the Executor box (and between Executor and the worker row) so each multi-line arrow label sits fully in clear space with padding above and below - no label should touch a box border. Pull the 'V1: dedicated process' pill fully inside the frame with right-margin so it never crosses the viewBox edge in either theme. Keep everything else; this is purely a spacing/legibility pass, not a redesign.

new_figures.name: vllm-scheduler-budget-preemption
new_figures.concept: The scheduler as a three-state machine running continuous batching under a fixed KV-block budget, with preemption (recompute vs swap) firing when the pool is full.
new_figures.rationale: This is the heart of the chapter's unique 'system' contribution and is currently unserved by any figure. Readers must intuit that KV blocks are a hard, shared budget; that requests flow WAITING->RUNNING->(finish or preempt); that a running sequence needs +1 block at a block boundary each step; and that when the budget is exhausted vLLM evicts a WHOLE sequence with two recovery paths - and that oversubscribing causes thrashing. A single well-labeled state diagram plus a budget meter makes all of this click in one frame.
new_figures.placement: The scheduler: continuous batching under a memory budget -> subsection 'The three queues and the step loop'
new_figures.brief: A single static infographic with two coupled parts. TOP: a horizontal 'KV block pool' meter - a long rounded bar divided into small equal cells (illustrative count, ~20 cells) with most cells shaded 'used' by a couple of colored sequences and a few cells 'free' at the right; label the bar 'Total KV blocks = hard ceiling (num_gpu_blocks). Every running sequence draws from this one pool.' MIDDLE/MAIN: three labeled lanes or nodes for the request states - WAITING ('admitted, no KV blocks yet'), RUNNING ('has KV blocks; in the next forward pass'), SWAPPED ('KV evicted to CPU RAM, V0 path'). Draw directed transitions with clear labels: WAITING -> RUNNING labeled 'admit: allocate prompt blocks (if budget allows)'; RUNNING -> RUNNING self-loop labeled 'each decode step: +1 block only at a block boundary'; RUNNING -> (leaves) labeled 'finish: free blocks back to pool'; and the two preemption paths drawn prominently when the pool is full: RUNNING -> WAITING labeled 'preempt by RECOMPUTE (default in V1): drop KV, re-prefill later - frees memory now, wastes compute', and RUNNING -> SWAPPED labeled 'preempt by SWAP (V0): copy KV to CPU, pay PCIe twice, copy back later'. Show a small 'POOL FULL' trigger badge on the budget meter connected to the preempt transitions to make causality explicit ('no free block -> evict a whole sequence, tail of the running list first'). BOTTOM: a one-line caption warning 'Oversubscribe the pool -> admit, decode, preempt, recompute, preempt again = thrashing: throughput collapses, p99 explodes (watch num_preemptions).' Use only two illustrative sequence colors from theme-safe accents; no invented benchmark numbers; small illustrative integers only. Must be fully readable as a static frame in both light and dark themes with no text touching the viewBox edge.

new_figures.name: vllm-prefix-cache-rolling-hash
new_figures.concept: Automatic prefix caching: a rolling per-block hash makes identical prefixes map to identical physical blocks, so a shared prefix is reused up to the first divergent block, then only the tail is prefilled.
new_figures.rationale: Prefix caching is a major, distinctive section and the single biggest lever for agents / RL rollouts, yet it has no figure. The rolling-hash-and-reuse mechanism is inherently visual and, once seen, makes the whole 'reuse falls out of the paged design + refcounting' argument obvious. It also sets up the correctness point (hash must cover LoRA id + tenant salt).
new_figures.placement: Prefix caching: reuse KV across requests -> subsection 'How it works: hashing blocks'
new_figures.brief: A static infographic comparing two requests that share a leading prefix. Show Request A (top) and Request B (bottom) each as a left-to-right sequence of full KV blocks. Blocks 0..k contain the SAME tokens (e.g. a shared system prompt) in both requests; after block k the two diverge (different tokens). For each block draw its computed hash as a small chained tag, emphasizing the ROLLING nature: hash(block_i) = f(hash(block_i-1), tokens_in_block_i), drawn as an arrow from each block's hash feeding into the next block's hash (a chain). Make it visually obvious that because the leading blocks are identical AND the chain is identical, blocks 0..k of A and B produce IDENTICAL hashes (draw them with matching color/shade and equal-sign or 'same hash' connectors), while the first divergent block onward produces DIFFERENT hashes (different shade, 'miss'). In the middle, draw a small 'hash -> physical block' table: the shared hashes map to the SAME physical blocks (one physical copy, refcount 2), illustrated by both A and B block-tables pointing at the same shared physical blocks up to block k. After the divergence point, B allocates FRESH physical blocks for its tail. Annotate the payoff: 'B reuses blocks 0..k for free (no compute, no new memory - just refcount++), and prefills ONLY its divergent tail.' Add a small correctness note badge: 'hash also folds in LoRA id + tenant cache-salt, so one tenant's KV is never served to another.' Only FULL blocks are hashable - make the last partial block visually distinct (dashed / 'not yet committed'). Theme-safe colors, small illustrative block counts, no invented numbers. Must read as a static frame in both themes.

new_figures.name: vllm-chunked-prefill-token-budget
new_figures.concept: The V1 unified per-step token budget: one forward pass packs a slice of a long prefill together with many 1-token decodes, curing head-of-line blocking that a prefill-only step causes.
new_figures.rationale: Chunked prefill and the V1 'unified token budget' are central to how V1 works and why inter-token latency stays smooth, but there is no visual. A before/after budget-bar contrast makes the head-of-line-blocking problem and its fix immediately legible, and connects to the roofline intuition (mixing prefill tokens into a memory-bound decode step raises arithmetic intensity).
new_figures.brief: A two-panel static contrast, each panel showing a single forward pass as a horizontal 'token budget' bar of fixed width (max_num_batched_tokens). LEFT panel 'Naive: prefill-only step': the whole bar is filled by ONE long prompt's prefill tokens (a solid block), and beneath it show several decoding requests stalled/waiting ('D1, D2, D3 wait a full step -> inter-token latency spikes for everyone'); label 'a long 32k prefill monopolizes the pass = head-of-line blocking'. RIGHT panel 'Chunked prefill (V1 default): unified token budget': the SAME bar is filled by a SMALL CHUNK of the long prefill (at most a chunk of max_num_batched_tokens) PLUS many single-token decodes packed alongside it (draw the prefill chunk as one colored segment and each decode as a thin 1-token cell in a different accent, several of them). Label 'one pass mixes a prefill slice + many 1-token decodes; the long prompt is processed a chunk per step, interleaved.' Add a short caption tying the two benefits: 'Smooths inter-token latency (decodes never wait a whole long prefill) AND raises arithmetic intensity (decode alone is memory-bound; adding prefill tokens fills the compute).' Optionally show the long prefill as a stack of chunks with only the top chunk highlighted 'this step' to convey chunk-per-step progress. Theme-safe accent colors, small illustrative token counts only, no invented benchmarks. Fully understandable as a static frame in both themes; nothing clipping the viewBox.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: only two figures cover a huge amount of system material. Both existing figures are structurally sound and worth keeping, but each has concrete legibility bugs to fix - the paged-mapping figure has low-contrast/clipped captions in dark mode, and the engine/executor stack has overlapping arrow labels and a right-edge-clipped pill. The three unserved core intuitions - the scheduler running continuous batching under a fixed KV-block budget with preemption (the chapter's signature 'system' idea), automatic prefix caching via rolling block hashes, and chunked prefill / the V1 unified token budget - each deserve one foundational static infographic. Speculative decoding and multi-LoRA are treated as plumbing here with their own dedicated chapters, so they do not warrant new figures in this reference. No long-animations are needed: each intuition resolves cleanly in a single well-labeled static frame.
